items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-5-1, SVFR operations in weather conditions less than basic VFR minima are authorized in the surface areas of Class B, Class C, Class D, and Class _____ airspace.
items.explanation: Per 7-5-1a, SVFR is authorized in the surface areas of Class B, Class C, Class D, and Class E airspace. Class A airspace is excluded because SVFR is a VFR operation and VFR is prohibited in Class A (per 7-1-1).

choices.text: E

items.question_text: Under 7-5-1b, what requirement must be met at an airport with an operating control tower before a fixed-wing SVFR clearance may be issued?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-1b, at locations with an operating control tower, the ground visibility must be at least 1 statute mile before a fixed-wing SVFR clearance can be issued. Helicopters are exempt from this minimum and may operate SVFR in less than 1 mile visibility.

choices.text: The pilot must have filed an SVFR flight plan at least 30 minutes before departure.
Ground visibility must be at least 1 statute mile.
The ceiling must be at least 500 feet above the airport elevation.
The pilot must demonstrate SVFR currency in the preceding 90 days.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-5-2a, SVFR flights may be approved only if arriving and departing _____ aircraft are not delayed.
items.explanation: Per 7-5-2a, SVFR has lower priority than IFR. The rule is: SVFR may be approved only if IFR aircraft are not delayed. If an SVFR clearance would delay an IFR arrival or departure, the SVFR request must be denied.

choices.text: IFR

items.question_text: Under 7-5-2b, if SVFR cannot be granted because of IFR traffic departing every 2 minutes, what must the controller tell the SVFR pilot?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-2b, when SVFR cannot be granted due to IFR traffic (departing every 2 minutes or arriving on final with the same interval), the controller must advise the pilot of the expected delay using: 'EXPECT (time) DELAY.' This is a precise phraseology requirement.

choices.text: SVFR NOT AUTHORIZED, ADVISE WHEN READY TO FILE IFR.
EXPECT (time) DELAY.
UNABLE SVFR DUE TO IFR TRAFFIC, STAND BY.
SVFR REQUEST DENIED, CONTACT CLEARANCE DELIVERY.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-3a, what types of separation must be applied between two SVFR fixed-wing aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-3a, nonradar or visual separation must be applied between: SVFR fixed-wing aircraft, SVFR fixed-wing and SVFR helicopters, and SVFR fixed-wing and IFR aircraft. Note that radar separation is excluded from SVFR fixed-wing operations.

choices.text: Radar or nonradar separation.
Nonradar or visual separation.
Visual separation only; nonradar is not applicable.
Radar, nonradar, or visual separation as appropriate.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-4, when applying vertical separation, why is an SVFR aircraft not assigned a fixed altitude to maintain?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-4, SVFR aircraft are not assigned fixed altitudes because they must remain clear of clouds. Instead, the controller clears them at or below an altitude that is at least 500 feet below any conflicting IFR traffic but not below the MSA. This gives the pilot the flexibility to maneuver to stay clear of clouds while maintaining vertical separation from IFR traffic.

choices.text: SVFR aircraft are not equipped with Mode C transponders, so altitude assignment is not possible.
Because SVFR aircraft must remain clear of clouds, a fixed altitude could force them into a cloud. They are cleared at or below an altitude to maintain flexibility.
Fixed altitudes are only used for IFR aircraft; SVFR uses visual altitude separation.
Because SVFR altitudes are assigned by the tower, not by approach control.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-4, what is the minimum vertical buffer between an SVFR aircraft and conflicting IFR traffic?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-4, the controller clears the SVFR aircraft at or below an altitude that is at least 500 feet below any conflicting IFR traffic, but not below the MSA. This 500-foot buffer provides vertical separation while allowing the SVFR pilot cloud-clearance flexibility.

choices.text: At least 1,000 feet below conflicting IFR traffic.
At least 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic.
At least 300 feet below conflicting IFR traffic.
There is no specific vertical buffer; the controller assigns any altitude below IFR traffic.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-5, what is the key condition for authorizing local SVFR operations for a specified period?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-5a, local SVFR operations may be authorized for a specified period if the aircraft can be recalled when traffic or weather conditions require. This recallability ensures that SVFR operations can be suspended immediately if IFR traffic needs priority or weather deteriorates.

choices.text: The pilot must file a flight plan for each individual landing and takeoff.
The aircraft must be capable of IFR flight if weather deteriorates.
The aircraft can be recalled when traffic or weather conditions require.
Operations must be limited to a single runway at the airport.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-5-6, authorize an aircraft to climb to VFR upon request if the only weather limitation is restricted _____.
items.explanation: Per 7-5-6, a climb-to-VFR clearance is authorized only when the sole weather limitation is restricted visibility. If ceilings are also low, a different clearance (such as IFR or continue SVFR) is required.

choices.text: visibility

items.question_text: Under 7-5-7, may a helicopter conduct SVFR operations when ground visibility is below 1 mile?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-7, 14 CFR part 91 does not prohibit helicopter SVFR flight when visibility is less than 1 mile. This is a key difference from fixed-wing SVFR operations, which require at least 1 mile ground visibility at towered airports (7-5-1b).

choices.text: No, helicopters require the same 1-mile visibility as fixed-wing aircraft.
Yes, 14 CFR part 91 does not prohibit helicopter SVFR flight when visibility is less than 1 mile.
Yes, but only for emergency medical services (EMS) operations.
No, helicopters must have at least 1/2 mile visibility for SVFR.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-8, what must the controller do for an arriving aircraft operating outside Class B, C, D, or E surface area when the pilot advises flight visibility is less than 1 mile?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-8, for an arriving aircraft outside Class B/C/D/E surface area, if the pilot advises flight visibility is less than 1 mile, the controller tells the pilot to remain clear of clouds and of the airport traffic area. This advisory is necessary because SVFR minimums cannot be met, but the aircraft is outside controlled airspace surface areas.

choices.text: Tell the pilot to land immediately at the nearest suitable airport.
Tell the pilot to remain clear of clouds and of the airport traffic area.
Issue an IFR clearance because SVFR is not available outside surface areas.
Authorize SVFR with the understanding that visibility minimums are waived.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-3a, between which pairs must nonradar or visual separation be applied during SVFR operations?

choices.text: Between SVFR helicopters and VFR aircraft, and between SVFR helicopters and IFR aircraft.
Only between SVFR fixed-wing aircraft and IFR aircraft.
Between SVFR fixed-wing and SVFR fixed-wing; SVFR fixed-wing and SVFR helicopters; and SVFR fixed-wing and IFR aircraft.
Between all SVFR and all VFR aircraft within the airport traffic area.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-3a, nonradar or visual separation must be applied between: (1) SVFR fixed-wing aircraft, (2) SVFR fixed-wing and SVFR helicopters, and (3) SVFR fixed-wing and IFR aircraft. Note that SVFR helicopter-to-SVFR helicopter separation is not listed.

items.situation: An SVFR fixed-wing aircraft needs vertical separation from an IFR aircraft at 3,000 feet MSL. The MSA for the area is 1,500 feet MSL. The ceiling is 1,200 feet overcast.
items.para_context: Per 7-5-4, do not assign a fixed altitude. Clear the SVFR aircraft at or below an altitude which is at least 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic but not below the MSA. The pilot must also remain clear of clouds.
items.question_text: What clearance should the controller issue?

choices.text: MAINTAIN AT OR BELOW 2,500 (500 ft below IFR at 3,000).
MAINTAIN 2,500 (a fixed altitude to ensure vertical separation).
MAINTAIN AT OR BELOW 1,000 (to stay below the 1,200 ft ceiling).
CLIMB AND MAINTAIN 3,500 (above the IFR traffic).

items.explanation: Per 7-5-4, the SVFR aircraft gets an 'at or below' restriction at 500 ft below the IFR aircraft (2,500 ft) which is above the MSA (1,500 ft), giving the pilot room to stay clear of clouds (ceiling 1,200 ft) while maintaining separation from IFR traffic.

items.instruction: The controller authorized a climb-to-VFR clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CLIMB TO VFR, MAINTAIN SPECIAL VFR CONDITIONS UNTIL REACHING VFR.

choices.text: Climb to VFR, maintaining special VFR conditions until we're in VMC.
Climb to VFR, maintain special VFR conditions until reaching VFR.
Climbing to VFR, canceling special VFR now.
Climb to VFR, we'll maintain IFR until reaching VMC.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-6, the aircraft maintains special VFR conditions until reaching VFR. The pilot must stay in SVFR throughout the climb through restricted visibility.

items.situation: Ground visibility is officially reported at 3/4 mile at a towered airport. A fixed-wing aircraft requests SVFR departure. The aircraft is not a scheduled air carrier.
items.para_context: Per 7-5-7, when ground visibility is below 1 mile, advise the pilot of the visibility and ask intentions. Scheduled air carriers may not be authorized SVFR unless the pilot indicates an emergency.
items.question_text: How should the controller handle this fixed-wing SVFR departure request?

choices.text: Deny the request because ground visibility is below 1 mile.
Advise the pilot of the visibility and ask the pilot's intentions.
Authorize SVFR immediately because the visibility is below 1 mile and the pilot has already requested it.
Issue a special VFR clearance only if the aircraft has filed a flight plan.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-7, when ground visibility is below 1 mile for fixed-wing operations, tell the pilot the visibility and ask intentions. The pilot can then decide whether to proceed knowing the visibility is below the normal SVFR minimum.

items.question_text: Under 7-5-5, when may an FSS be authorized to transmit SVFR clearances for local operations?

choices.text: When the FSS has direct communication with the controlling facility and an LOA is established.
Any time an FSS receives a pilot request for SVFR, because FSS has independent clearance authority.
Only for helicopter operations at uncontrolled airports.
When the airport is below VFR minimums and no tower is in operation.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-5, where warranted and LOAs are established, FSS may be authorized to transmit local SVFR clearances. This requires both a direct communication link and an established LOA.
